summary:
Taforalt Cave (also known as Grotte des Pigeons) is a prehistoric rock shelter in the Beni Snassen mountains of northeastern Morocco, with an occupation sequence extending from approximately 23,000 to 10,000 BCE. The cave is one of the most significant Epipaleolithic sites in North Africa and is particularly renowned for its large cemetery — one of the largest prehistoric burial grounds in the continent — containing the remains of over 30 individuals associated with the Iberomaurusian (Oranian) cultural tradition.

The Taforalt human remains have been central to genetic and isotopic studies of North African prehistory. Genomic analysis of the Taforalt individuals, published in 2018, revealed an unexpected genetic affinity with sub-Saharan African populations — specifically related to West Africans — in addition to Eurasian ancestry, suggesting complex and ancient population movements across North Africa during and after the Last Glacial Maximum. The Taforalt people produced personal ornaments including numerous perforated shells (Nassarius gibbosulus) used as beads, some of the oldest personal ornaments known in the world, linking the site to broader North African and Levantine traditions of symbolic body adornment extending back more than 130,000 years. By the period 10,000 BCE, the last inhabitants of Taforalt Cave were likely contemporaries of the earliest [[event:1293|Capsian culture]] communities in the eastern Maghreb.
